current_fact.fact_text: Threaded through these May 17 stories is a quieter pattern: technology misfires, borders move, cultures clash and then sing. From robotic arcades in California to flamenco in Madrid and Afrobeat in Kinshasa’s wake, the same date keeps asking: what do we build, what do we break, and what music rises from it?

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

current_fact.match_reason: A closing, big‑picture reflection over the day’s events fits with Sedna FM’s very first, wide‑ranging ‘life soundtrack’ episode, tying many stories and genres into one origin.

published.fact_text: In 1969, the Soviet probe Venera 6 plunged into Venus’s crushing atmosphere, transmitting precious data until the pressure literally crushed it. For a brief window, a human-made object was breathing in an alien sky 50 times denser than Earth’s before going silent forever.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space exploration milestone pairs naturally with an episode themed around ‘space’ and shifting perspectives beyond Earth.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1983, the U.S. Department of Energy quietly acknowledged that Oak Ridge, Tennessee, had suffered what became known as the world’s largest mercury pollution event—over 4.2 million pounds released. The revelation only surfaced because a small regional newspaper insisted on digging through Freedom of Information Act requests.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A story about hidden environmental damage and institutional silence fits with an episode about becoming more deliberate and attentive to what we consume.

published.fact_text: In 2006, the decommissioned aircraft carrier USS Oriskany was deliberately sunk in the Gulf of Mexico to become an artificial reef. The 276-meter warship now serves as a steel skyscraper for marine life and one of the world’s largest purpose-sunk dive sites.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A sunken carrier turned dive site aligns perfectly with a water‑and‑immersion themed episode about going below the surface.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1673, Louis Jolliet and Jesuit priest Jacques Marquette set off to chart the Mississippi River, guided largely by Indigenous knowledge and oral maps. Their journey helped Europeans redraw their mental map of North America, revealing a vast watery highway that had been central to Native life for centuries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A river exploration story resonates with an episode framed as a boat voyage across waters and cultures.

published.fact_text: In 1809, Napoleon ordered the annexation of the Papal States, effectively shrinking the Pope’s temporal power and folding centuries-old territories into the French Empire. The move turned a theological authority into a reluctant political pawn in the continental chessboard.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A power struggle over Italian lands fits with a reflective Italian‑rooted episode about landscapes, borders and how they shape stories.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1987, an Iraqi Mirage F1 mistakenly fired two Exocet missiles into the USS Stark, killing 37 sailors in minutes. The incident became a chilling example of how modern warfare’s split‑second errors can rewrite diplomatic and human lives alike.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A tragic wartime misfire matches with a politically charged, anti‑war‑leaning rock episode critiquing militarism and power.

published.fact_text: On the eve of Peru’s 1980 elections, the Maoist group Shining Path burned ballot boxes in the small town of Chuschi, marking the symbolic start of a violent internal conflict. The war that followed would last two decades and leave tens of thousands of Peruvians dead or missing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A Latin American insurgency and political violence align with an episode centered on a fiercely political Latin artist confronting power and conflict.

published.fact_text: Delta Air Lines Flight 318 crashed near Marshall, Texas, in 1953 after encountering severe weather and losing control on approach, killing 19 people. The investigation further underscored how quickly storms can turn controlled flight into chaos in the pre‑jet age.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A story of fragile human flight and risk pairs with a reflective, cinematic episode about traversing dangerous environments with awe and caution.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1997, Laurent-Désiré Kabila’s forces marched into Kinshasa and erased the name ‘Zaire’ from maps, renaming the country the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It was a rebranding of a nation scarred by dictatorship, hoping to reclaim an identity older than the colonial labels imposed on it.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: An African nation’s political rebirth pairs with an episode celebrating African musical revolutions and the reclaiming of cultural identity.

published.fact_text: In 1977, Atari co-founder Nolan Bushnell opened the first Chuck E. Cheese’s Pizza Time Theatre in San Jose, California, to fuse arcade games, pizza, and animatronic concerts into one family venue. It became a strange cultural crossroads where early video gaming, children’s parties, and robotic mascots all evolved together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: A retro arcade‑pizza chain fits with a nostalgia‑drenched episode about childhood/teen entertainment and playful pop culture.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1967, Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser demanded the withdrawal of the UN Emergency Force from Sinai, removing a buffer between Egyptian and Israeli armies. Within weeks, the Six-Day War would redraw borders and rewrite geopolitics in West Asia in less than a week.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

published.match_reason: A pivotal Middle Eastern/North African political moment pairs with an episode centered on North African sounds and histories.

published.fact_text: In 1940, German forces occupied Brussels as part of their swift invasion of Belgium, bringing World War II’s frontline into the heart of a historic European capital. The city’s layered history suddenly had to rewrite itself under the shadow of occupation, resistance, and daily survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: The contrast between wartime occupation and the idea of tranquil summers invites reflection on how peace and conflict reshape the same European landscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1648, a joint French–Swedish army defeated Imperial and Bavarian forces at the Battle of Zusmarshausen, one of the last clashes of the Thirty Years’ War. By then, an entire generation had grown up knowing only a Europe defined by religiously justified war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A brutal religious war and its stubborn hierarchies pair thematically with an episode about defiance, challenging entrenched power and rewriting roles.

published.fact_text: In 1527, Pánfilo de Narváez sailed from Spain with about 600 men to explore Florida; by 1536, only four survivors remained. Their trek across what is now the American South produced some of the earliest European descriptions of regions like Texas and the Gulf Coast—written by people who had lost almost everything.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A perilous expedition into unfamiliar lands echoes a physically demanding journey to new territories of body and mind in a distant country.

published.fact_text: In 1012, the powerful Counts of Tusculum maneuvered to have the count’s son elevated as Pope Sergius IV, blending noble family strategy with spiritual authority. It’s a reminder that for much of history, the papacy was as much about dynastic politics as divine calling.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A story of power, lineage, and spiritual symbolism pairs with a reflective, place‑anchored episode about how certain lands and titles carry deep, almost mystical weight.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1995, a man named Shawn Nelson stole an M60 tank from a National Guard armory in San Diego and drove it through city streets, crushing cars and infrastructure for 23 minutes. The surreal spectacle looked like a war zone unspooling in suburban America, until police managed to stop the 57‑ton machine.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The chaotic, high‑energy spectacle of a tank rampage fits with an intense, electrifying rock episode that feels like a sonic jolt.

published.fact_text: In 1984, Prince Charles publicly condemned a proposed modernist extension to London’s National Gallery as a “monstrous carbuncle on the face of a much-loved and elegant friend.” The phrase ignited a nationwide debate about architecture, taste, and how much royalty should shape the city’s skyline.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

published.match_reason: A London‑centric controversy about art and modernity pairs with a London‑rooted episode celebrating the city’s evolving cultural landscape.

published.fact_text: In 1980, Miami erupted into days of rioting after a jury acquitted four white police officers in the killing of Arthur McDuffie, a Black insurance salesman beaten during a traffic stop. The unrest exposed deep racial tensions in a city marketed as a sunny paradise.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A story about racial injustice and public rage resonates with a conscious‑rap‑driven episode examining identity, oppression, and breaking out of imposed boxes.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1937, Spain’s Largo Caballero government resigned after violent clashes in Barcelona known as the May Days, and Juan Negrín formed a new government without the anarcho-syndicalist CNT. The shift signaled deep fractures within the anti‑fascist camp during the Spanish Civil War.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A pivotal Spanish Civil War political shift pairs with an episode exploring Spain’s cultural soul music, linking history, conflict, and artistic expression.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 2007, trains crossed the Demilitarized Zone between North and South Korea for the first time since 1953 in a carefully negotiated test run. For a few hours, steel tracks briefly overrode one of the world’s most militarized borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A tentative reconnection after decades of division mirrors an episode about slow, patient progress and the hopeful work of building new pathways.

published.fact_text: In 1969, the Venera 6 probe’s descent into Venus, Oak Ridge’s toxic legacy, and Oriskany’s transformation into a reef all show different ways humans leave traces on other worlds and their own oceans. Each May 17 reminder hints that exploration, industry, and war can all reshape environments in ways we’re only just beginning to understand.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: This meta‑reflection on planetary footprints fits with a dusk‑like, slowly expanding global mix that moves from intimate spaces to wide, planetary soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 17, 1980, the attack in Chuschi wasn’t the only turning point: the same date sees Miami’s Arthur McDuffie verdict and the simmering Spanish May Days in historical memory. Taken together, they map a day where questions of justice, state power, and who gets to be heard echo across continents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: A reflection on protests, power, and public space connects to an episode about how another kind of public space—the dancefloor—can be reclaimed for empowerment and collective presence.

published.fact_text: Across a single May 17, explorers chart rivers, ships become reefs, papal crowns shift heads, and border trains move for the first time in decades. It’s a reminder that history doesn’t advance in one direction; it sprawls, doubling back through oceans, deserts, cathedrals, and DMZs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A zoomed‑out view of many journeys and transformations fits with a travel‑themed, perspective‑shifting episode built as a moving meditation across places.
